Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - Unpacking Information Bottlenecks: Surrogate Objec tives for Deep Learning

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## Abstract

The Information Bottleneck principle offers both a mechanism to explain how deep neural networks train and generalize, as well as a regularized objective with which to train models. However, multiple competing objectives are proposed in the literature, and the information-theoretic quantities used in these objectives are difficult to compute for large deep neural networks, which in turn limits their use as a training objective. In this work, we review these quantities, and compare and unify previously proposed objectives, which allows us to develop surrogate objectives more friendly to optimization without relying on cumbersome tools such as density estimation. We find that these surrogate objectives allow us to apply the information bottleneck to modern neural network architectures. We demonstrate our insights on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and Imagenette with modern DNN architectures (ResNets).

## 1 Introduction

The Information Bottleneck (IB) principle, introduced by Tishby et al. (2000), proposes that training and generalization in deep neural networks (DNNs) can be explained by information-theoretic principles (Tishby and Zaslavsky, 2015; Shwartz-Ziv and Tishby, 2017; Achille and Soatto, 2018a). This is attractive as the success of DNNs remains largely unexplained by tools from computational learning theory (Zhang et al., 2016; Bengio et al., 2009). The IB principle suggests that learning consists of two competing objectives: maximizing the mutual information between the latent representation and the label to promote accuracy, while at the same time minimizing the mutual information between the latent representation and the input to promote generalization. Following this principle, many variations of IB objectives have been proposed (Alemi et al., 2016; Strouse and Schwab, 2017; Fischer and Alemi, 2020; Fischer, 2020; Fisher, 2019; Gondek and Hofmann, 2003; Achille and Soatto, 2018a), which, in supervised learning, have been demonstrated to benefit robustness to adversarial attacks (Alemi et al., 2016; Fisher, 2019) and generalization and regularization against overfitting to random labels (Fisher, 2019).

Whether the benefits of training with IB _objectives_ are due to the IB _principle_ , or some other unrelated mechanism, remains unclear (Saxe et al., 2019; Amjad and Geiger, 2019; Tschannen et al., 2019), suggesting that although recent work has also tied the principle to successful results in both unsupervised and self-supervised learning (Oord et al., 2018; Belghazi et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Burgess et al., 2018, among others), our understanding of how IB objectives affect representation learning remains unclear.

Critical to studying this question is the computation of the information-theoretic quantities[1] used. While progress has been made in developing mutual information estimators for DNNs (Poole et al., 2019; Belghazi et al., 2018; Noshad et al., 2019; McAllester and Stratos, 2018; Kraskov et al., 2004), current methods still face many limitations when concerned with high-dimensional random variables (McAllester and Stratos, 2018) and rely on complex estimators or generative models. This presents a challenge to training with IB objectives.

In this paper, we analyze information quantities and relate them to surrogate objectives for the IB principle which are more friendly to optimization, showing that complex or intractable IB objectives can be replaced with simple, easy-to-compute surrogates that produce similar performance and similar

behaviour of information quantities over training. **Sections 2** & **3** review commonly-used information quantities for which we provide mathematically grounded intuition via information diagrams and unify different IB objectives by identifying two key information quantities, Decoder Uncertainty H[Y | Z] and Reverse Decoder Uncertainty H[Z | Y] which act as the main loss and regularization terms in our unified IB objective. In particular, **Section 3.2** demonstrates that using the Decoder Uncertainty as a training objective can minimize the training error, and shows how to estimate an upper bound on it efficiently for well-known DNN architectures. We expand on the findings of Alemi et al. (2016) in their variational IB approximation and demonstrate that this upper bound is equal to the commonly-used cross-entropy loss[2] under dropout regularization. **Section 3.3** examines pathologies of differential entropies that hinder optimization and proposes adding Gaussian noise to force differential entropies to become non-negative, which leads to new surrogate terms to optimize the Reverse Decoder Uncertainty. Altogether this leads to simple and tractable surrogate IB objectives such as the following, which uses dropout, adds Gaussian noise over the feature vectors _f_ ( _x_ ; η), and uses an L2 penalty over the noisy feature vectors:

**Section 4** describes experiments that validate our insights qualitatively and quantitatively on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and Imagenette, and shows that with objectives like the one in equation (1) we obtain information plane plots (as in figure 1) similar to those predicted by Tishby and Zaslavsky (2015). Our simple surrogate objectives thus induce the desired behavior of IB objectives while scaling to large, high-dimensional datasets. We present **evaluations on CIFAR-10 and Imagenette images**[3] .

Compared to existing work, we show that we can optimize IB objectives for well-known DNN architectures using standard optimizers, losses and simple regularizers, without needing complex estimators, generative models, or variational approximations. This will allow future research to make better use of IB objectives and study the IB principle more thoroughly.

## 2 Background

**Information quantities** & **information diagrams.** We denote entropy H[·], joint entropy H[·, ·], conditional entropy H[· | ·], mutual information I[·; ·] and Shannon’s information content _h_ (·) (Cover and Thomas, 2012; MacKay, 2003; Shannon, 1948). We will further require the Kullback-Leibler divergence _D_ KL(· || ·) and cross-entropy H(· || ·). The definitions can be found in section A.1. We will use differential entropies interchangeably with entropies: equalities between them are preserved in the differential setting, and inequalities will be covered in section 3.3.

> 2This connection was assumed without proof by Achille and Soatto (2018a;b).

> 3Recently, Fischer and Alemi (2020) report results on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet, see section F.4.

Information diagrams (I-diagrams), like the one depicted in figure 2, clarify the relationship between information quantities: _similar to Venn diagrams, a quantity equals the sum of its parts in the diagram_ . Importantly, they offer a grounded intuition as Yeung (1991) show that we can define a signed measure µ[*] such that information quantities map to abstract sets and are consistent with set operations. We provide details on how to use I-diagrams and what to watch out for in section A.2.

**Probabilistic model.** We will focus on a supervised classification task that makes prediction Y[ˆ] given data X using a latent encoding Z, while the provided target is Y. We assume categorical Y and ˆY, and continuous X. Our probabilistic model based on these assumptions is as follows:

p( _x_ , _y_ , _z_ , ˆ _y_ ) = ˆp( _x_ , _y_ ) pθ( _z_ | _x_ ) pθ(ˆ _y_ | _z_ ). (2)

Thus, Z and Y are independent given X, and Y[ˆ] is independent of X and Y given Z. The data distribution ˆp( _x_ , _y_ ) is only available to us as an empirical sample distribution. θ are the parameters we would like to learn. pθ( _z_ | _x_ ) is the encoder from data X to latent Z, and pθ( _y_ ˆ | _z_ ) the decoder from latent Z to prediction Y.[ˆ] Together, pθ( _z_ | _x_ ) and pθ(ˆ _y_ | _z_ ) form the discriminative model pθ(ˆ _y_ | _x_ ):

pθ(ˆ _y_ | _x_ ) = E pθ( _z_ | _x_ ) pθ(ˆ _y_ | _z_ ). (3)

We can derive the cross-entropy loss H(ˆp( _y_ | _x_ ) || pθ(Y[ˆ] = _y_ | _x_ )) (Solla et al., 1988; Hinton, 1990) by minimizing the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the empirical sample distribution ˆp( _x_ , _y_ ) and the parameterized distribution pθ( _x_ ) pθ(ˆ _y_ | _x_ ), where we set pθ( _x_ ) = ˆp( _x_ ). See section D.1.

**Mickey Mouse I-diagram.** The corresponding I-diagram for X, Y, and Z is depicted in figure 2. As some of the quantities have been labelled before, we try to follow conventions and come up with consistent names otherwise. _Section B.1 provides intuitions for these quantities, and section B.2 lists all definitions and equivalences explicitly_ . For categorical Z, all the quantities in the diagram are positive, which allows us to read off inequalities from the diagram: only I[X; Y; Z] could be negative, but as Y and Z are independent given X, we have I[Y; Z|X] = 0, and I[X; Y; Z] = I[Y; Z]−I[Y; Z|X] = I[Y; Z] ≥ 0. Section 3.3 investigates how to preserve inequalities for continuous Z.

## 3 Surrogate IB & DIB objectives

## 3.1 IB Objectives

Tishby et al. (2000) introduce the IB objective as a relaxation of a constrained optimization problem: minimize the mutual information between the input _X_ and its latent representation _Z_ while still accurately predicting _Y_ from _Z_ . An analogous objective which yields deterministic _Z_ , the Deterministic Information Bottleneck (DIB) was proposed by Strouse and Schwab (2017). Letting β be a Lagrange multiplier, we arrive at the IB and DIB objectives:

min I[X; Z] − βI[Y; Z] for IB, and min H[Z] − βI[Y; Z] for DIB. (4) This principle can be recast as a generalization of finding minimal sufficient statistics for the labels given the data (Shamir et al., 2010; Tishby and Zaslavsky, 2015; Fisher, 2019): it strives for minimality and sufficiency of the latent Z. Minimality is achieved by minimizing the Preserved Information I[X; Z]; while sufficiency is achieved by maximizing the Preserved Relevant Information I[Y; Z]. We defer an in-depth discussion of the IB principle to the appendix Section C.1. We discuss the several variants of IB objectives, and justify our focus on IB and DIB, in Section C.2.

The information quantities that appear in the IB objective are not tractable to compute for the representations learned by many function classes of interest, including neural networks; for example, Strouse and Schwab (2017) only obtain an analytical solution to their Deterministic Information Bottleneck (DIB) method for the tabular setting. Alemi et al. (2016) address this challenge by constructing a variational approximation of the IB objective, but their approach has not been applied to more complex datasets than MNIST variants. Belghazi et al. (2018) use a separate statistics network to approximate the mutual information, a computationally expensive strategy that does not easily lend itself to optimization.

In this section, we introduce and justify tractable surrogate losses that are easier to apply in common deep learning pipelines, and which can be scaled to large and high-dimensional datasets. We begin by proposing the following reformulation of IB and DIB objectives.

In the next sections, we show that Decoder Uncertainty H[Y | Z] provides a loss term, which minimizes the training error, and DIB’s Reverse Decoder Uncertainty H[Z | Y] and IB’s Redundant Information I[X; Z | Y], respectively, provide a regularization term, which helps generalization. Another perspective can be found by relating the objectives to the Entropy Distance Metric introduced by MacKay (2003), which we detail in section C.4.

## 3.2 Decoder Uncertainty H[Y | Z]

The Decoder Uncertainty H[Y | Z] is the first term in our reformulated IB and DIB objectives, and captures the data fit component of the IB principle. This quantity is not easy to compute directly for arbitrary representations _Z_ , so we turn our attention to two related entities instead, where we use θ as subscript to mark dependence on the model: the Prediction Cross-Entropy, denoted Hθ[Y | X] (more commonly known as the model’s cross-entropy loss; see section D.1), and the Decoder Cross-Entropy, denoted Hθ[Y | Z]. Noting that _h_ ( _x_ ) = − ln _x_ , we define these terms as follows:

Jensen’s inequality yields Hθ[Y | X] ≤ Hθ[Y | Z], with equality iff _Z_ is a deterministic function of _X_ . The notational similarity[4] between Hθ[Y | Z] and H[Y | Z] is deliberately suggestive: this cross-entropy bounds the conditional entropy H[Y | Z], as characterized in the following proposition.

Alemi et al. (2016) use the Decoder Cross-Entropy bound in equation (9) to variationally approximate p( _y_ | _z_ ). Equation (11) can be applied to many forms of stochastic regularization that turn deterministic models into stochastic ones, in particular dropout. This allows us to use modern DNN architectures as stochastic encoders.

**Dropout regularization** When we interpret η as a sampled dropout mask for a DNN, DNNs that use dropout regularization (Srivastava et al., 2014), or variants like DropConnect (Wan et al., 2013a), fit the equation above as stochastic encoders. Monte-Carlo dropout (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016), for example, even specifically estimates the predictive mean pθ( _y_ ˆ | _x_ ) from equation (3). The following result extends the observation by Burda et al. (2015) that sampling yields an unbiased estimator for the Decoder Cross-Entropy Hθ[Y | Z], while it only yields a biased estimator for the Prediction Cross-Entropy Hθ[Y | X] (which it upper-bounds).

This distinction between the Decoder Cross-Entropy and the Prediction Cross-Entropy has been observed in passing in the literature, but not made explicit. Multi-sample approaches like Multi-Sample Dropout (Inoue, 2019), for example, optimize Hθ[Y | Z], while Importance Weighted Stochastic Gradient Descent (Noh et al., 2017) optimizes Hθ[Y | X]. Dusenberry et al. (2020) observe empirically in the different context of rank-1 Bayesian Neural Networks that optimizing Hθ[Y | Z] instead of Hθ[Y | X] is both easier and also yields better generalization performance (NLL, accuracy, and ECE), while they also put forward an argument for why the stochastic gradients for Hθ[Y | Z] might benefit from lower variance. We empirically compare training with either cross-entropy in section G.3.3 and show results in figure G.11 in the appendix. We conclude this section by highlighting that H[Y | Z] is therefore already minimized in modern DNN architectures that use dropout together with a cross-entropy loss. This means that, at least for one half of our reformulation of the IB objective, we can apply off-the-shelf, scalable objectives and optimizers for its minimization.

## 3.3 Surrogates for the regularization terms

In the previous section, we have examined how to tractably estimate the error minimization term H[Y | Z]. In this section, we will examine tractable optimization of the regularization terms H[Z | Y] and I[X; Z | Y], respectively. We discuss how to minimize entropies meaningfully and show how this unifies DIB and IB via the inequality I[X; Z | Y] ≤ H[Z | Y] ≤ H[Z] before providing tractable upper-bounds for H[Z | Y] and H[Z].

**Di** ff **erential entropies** In most cases, the latent Z is a continuous random variable in many dimensions. Unlike entropies on discrete probability spaces, differential entropies defined on continuous spaces are not bounded from below. This means that the DIB objective is not guaranteed to have an optimal solution and allows for pathological optimization trajectories in which the variance of the latent _Z_ can be scaled to be arbitrarily small, achieving arbitrarily high-magnitude negative entropy. We provide a toy experiment demonstrating this in section G.4.

Intuitively, one can interpret this issue as being allowed to encode information in an arbitrarily-small real number using infinite precision, similar to arithmetic coding (MacKay, 2003; Shwartz-Ziv and Tishby, 2017)[5] . In practice, due to floating point constraints, optimizing DIB naively will invariably end in garbage predictions and underflow as activations approach zero. It is therefore not desirable

However, here we propose adding specific noise to the latent representation to lower-bound the conditional entropy of _Z_ , which allows us to enforce non-negativity across all IB information quantities as in the discrete case and transport inequalities to the continuous case: for a continuous ˆZ ∈ R _[k]_ and independent noise ϵ, we set Z := ˆZ + ϵ; the differential entropy then satisfies H[Z] = H[ˆZ + ϵ] ≥ H[ϵ]; and by using _zero-entropy noise_ ϵ ∼N(0, 21π _e[I][k]_[) specifically, we obtain][ H[Z]][ ≥][H][[][ϵ][]][ =][ 0][.] **Proposition 3.** _After adding zero-entropy noise, the inequality_ I[X; Z | Y] ≤ H[Z | Y] ≤ H[Z] _also holds for continuous_ Z _, and we can minimize_ I[X; Z | Y] _in the IB objective by minimizing_ H[Z | Y] _or_ H[Z] _, similarly to the DIB objective._

Strictly speaking, zero-entropy noise is not necessary for optimizing the bounds: any Gaussian noise is sufficient, but zero-entropy noise is aesthetically appealing as it preserves inequalities from the discrete setting. In a sense, this propostion bounds the IB objective by the DIB objective. However, adding noise changes the optimal solutions: whereas DIB in Strouse and Schwab (2017) leads to hard clustering in the limit, adding noise leads to soft clustering when optimizing the DIB objective, as is the case with the IB objective. We show in section F.6 that minimizing the DIB objective with noise leads to soft clustering (for the case of an otherwise deterministic encoder). Altogether, in addition to Shwartz-Ziv and Tishby (2017), we argue that noise is essential to obtain meaningful differential entropies and to avoid other pathological cases as described further in section F.7.

It is not generally possible to compute H[Z | Y] exactly for continuous latent representations _Z_ , but we can derive an upper bound. The maximum-entropy distribution for a given covariance matrix Σ is a Gaussian with the same covariance.

We refer to them as: conditional log-variance regularizer (log Var[Z | Y]), log-variance regularizer (log Var[Z]) and activation _L_ 2 regularizer ( E ∥ _Z_ ∥[2] ). ## 4 Experiments

We now provide empirical verification of the claims made in the previous sections. Our goal in this section is to highlight two main findings: first, that our surrogate objectives obtain similar behavior to what we expect of exact IB objectives with respect to their effect on robustness to adversarial examples. In particular, we show that our surrogate IB objectives improve adversarial robustness compared to models trained only on the cross-entropy loss, consistent with the findings of Alemi et al. (2016). Second, we show the effect of our surrogate objectives on information quantities during training by plotting information plane diagrams, demonstrating that models trained with our objectives trade off between _I_ [ _X_ ; _Z_ ] and _I_ [ _Y_ ; _Z_ ] as expected. We show this by recovering information plane plots similar to the ones in Tishby and Zaslavsky (2015) and qualitatively examine the optimization behavior of the networks through their training trajectories. We demonstrate the scalability of our surrogate objectives by applying our surrogate IB objectives to the CIFAR-10 and Imagenette datasets, high-dimensional image datasets.

For details about our experiment setup, DNN architectures, hyperparameters and additional insights, see section G. In particular, empirical quantification of our observations on the relationship between the Decoder Cross-Entropy loss and the Prediction Cross-Entropy are deferred to the appendix due to space limitations as well as the description of the toy experiment that shows that minimizing H[Z | Y] for continuous latent Z without adding noise does not constrain information meaningfully and that adding noise solves the issue as detailed in section 3.3.

**Robustness to adversarial attacks** Alemi et al. (2016) and Fischer and Alemi (2020) observe that their IB objectives lead to improved adversarial robustness over standard training objectives. We perform a similar evaluation to see whether our surrogate objectives also see improved robustness. We train a fully-connected residual network on CIFAR-10 for a range of regularization coefficients γ using our E ∥ _Z_ ∥[2] surrogate objective; we then compare against a similar regularization method that does not have an information-theoretic interpretation: L2 weight-decay. We inject zero-entropy noise in both cases. After training, we evaluate the models on adversarially perturbed images using the FGSM (Szegedy et al., 2013), PGD (Madry et al., 2018), BasicIterative (Kurakin et al., 2017) and DeepFool (Moosavi-Dezfooli et al., 2016) attacks for varying levels of the perturbation magnitude parameter ϵ. We also compare to a simple unregularized cross-entropy baseline (black dashed line). To compute overall robustness, we use each attack in turn and only count a sample as robust if it defeats them all. As depicted in figure 5, we find that our surrogate objectives yield significantly more robust models while obtaining similar test accuracy on the unperturbed data whereas weight-decay regularization reduces robustness against adversarial attacks. Plots for the other two regularizers can be found in the appendix in figure G.13 and figure G.14.

> **Information plane plots for CIFAR-10** To compare the different surrogate regularizers, we again use a ResNet18 model on CIFAR-10 with zero-entropy noise added to the final layer activations Z, with _K_ = 256 dimensions, as an encoder and add a single _K_ × 10 linear unit as a decoder. We train with the surrogate objectives from section 3.3 for various γ, chosen in logspace from different ranges to compensate for their relationship to β as noted in section 3.3: for log Var[Z], γ ∈ [10[−][5] , 1]; for log Var[Z | Y], γ ∈ [10[−][5] , 10]; and for E ∥ _Z_ ∥[2] , by trial and error, γ ∈ [10[−][6] , 10]. We estimate information quantities using the method of Kraskov et al. (2004).

Figure 6 shows an information plane plot for regularizing with E ∥ _Z_ ∥[2] for different γ over different epochs for the training set. Similar to Shwartz-Ziv and Tishby (2017), we observe that there is an initial expansion phase followed by compression. The jumps in performance (reduction of the Residual Information) are due to drops in the learning rate. In figure 4, we can see that the saturation curves for all 3 surrogate objectives qualitatively match the predicted curve from Tishby and Zaslavsky (2015). Figure G.1 shows the difference between the regularizers more clearly, and figure G.3 shows the training trajectories for all three regularizers. More details in section G.3.1.

**Information plane plots for Imagenette** To show that our surrogate objectives also scale up to larger datasets, we run a similar experiment on Imagenette (Howard, 2019), which is a subset of ImageNet with 10 classes with 224 × 224 × 3 = 1.5 × 10[5] input dimensions, and on which we obtain 90% test accuracy. See the figure 1, which shows the trajectories on the test set. We obtain similar plots to the ones obtained for CIFAR-10, _showing that our surrogate objectives scale well to higher-dimensional datasets despite their simplicity._

## 5 Conclusion

The contributions of this paper have been threefold: First, we have proposed simple, tractable training objectives which capture many of the desirable properties of IB methods while also scaling to problems of interest in deep learning. For this we have introduced implicit stochastic encoders, e.g. using dropout, and compared multi-sample dropout approaches to identify the one that approximates the Decoder Uncertainty Hθ[Y | Z], relating them to the cross-entropy loss that is commonly used for classification problems. This widens the range of DNN architectures that can be used with IB objectives considerably. We have demonstrated that our objectives perform well for practical DNNs without cumbersome density models. Second, we have motivated our objectives by providing insight into limitations of IB training, demonstrating how to avoid pathological behavior in IB objectives, and by endeavouring to provide a unifying view on IB approaches. Third, we have provided mathematically grounded intuition by using I-diagrams for the information quantities involved in IB, shown common pitfalls when using information quantities and how to avoid them, and examined how the quantities relate to each other. Future work investigating the practical constraints on the expressivity of a given neural network may provide further insight into how to measure compression in neural networks. Moreover, the connection to Bayesian Neural Networks remains to be explored.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This work demonstrates strong promise by proposing a practical, simplified framework for implementing the Information Bottleneck (IB) principle in large-scale deep learning, successfully pivoting away from the computationally prohibitive estimators that have historically hindered the field. The paper’s primary strength lies in its theoretical unification; by re-framing IB objectives through the lenses of "Decoder Uncertainty" and "Reverse Decoder Uncertainty," the authors offer a mathematically transparent path to optimization that intuitively bridges the gap between information theory and standard regularization techniques like dropout and L2 weight decay. While the clarity of the presentation—bolstered by the inclusion of I-diagrams—is commendable, the paper's most significant hurdle will be providing sufficient evidence to convince the reader that these "surrogate" objectives are not merely mimicking standard regularization, but are truly capturing the specific mechanistic benefits of the IB principle, especially given the existing skepticism in the literature regarding whether IB-driven generalization is distinct from traditional optimization dynamics. Addressing this theoretical nuance—and clarifying why these specific surrogates maintain the "information-theoretic flavor" of the original principle—will be essential to establishing the paper's contribution beyond a purely empirical refinement.

Specifically, the definitions of β' := β−1 and the resulting interval [0, infinity) conflict with standard IB parameterizations where β ∈ [0, 1]. Furthermore, the transition from the DIB objective to a surrogate form involving H[Y|Z] is mathematically underspecified and lacks the necessary derivation for the coefficient mapping, leaving the proposed reformulation ambiguous.

Claims that this transformation 'transports' discrete information-theoretic inequalities to continuous variables are not rigorously justified and mask the underlying complexity of information planes in continuous latent spaces.

comments.explanation: The authors simultaneously frame their proposed regularizers as surrogate objectives for the Information Bottleneck (IB) principle while asserting that the coefficients cannot be related to the original Lagrangian multipliers. comments.title: Inconsistency in Inequality for Conditional Mutual Information
comments.quote: I[X; Y; Z] = I[Y; Z]−I[Y; Z|X] = I[Y; Z] ≥ 0.
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors define a 'Mickey Mouse' I-diagram and state the equality I[X; Y; Z] = I[Y; Z] because I[Y; Z|X] = 0. While the identity for mutual information is broadly true under the provided Markov chain Z-X-Y, I[X; Y; Z] represents the 'interaction information' (or co-information), not the mutual information I[Y; Z]. Specifically, I[X; Y; Z] = I(X;Y) + I(X;Z) + I(Y;Z) - I(X,Y,Z). While their specific Markov assumption implies a simplification, the notation I[X; Y; Z] is conventionally used for the three-way interaction. Claiming it is equal to I[Y; Z] without qualification is highly unconventional and likely an error in applying I-diagram notation constraints.

Furthermore, the range for β' stated as [0, ∞) contradicts a standard inverse where β ∈ [0, 1] would map to [1, ∞). The definition of β'' similarly seems to contain a mismatch between the Lagrange multiplier interpretation and the resulting interval [0, 1). This is critical as it defines the surrogate objectives.

While the entropy of a Gaussian is related to its variance, the claim that this transformation makes the 'inequalities from the discrete setting' hold for continuous variables is an oversimplification. Differential entropy can be negative; adding continuous noise shifts the value but does not fundamentally turn it into a discrete-like probability measure. The jump to 'transporting inequalities' is not rigorously justified and risks misleading the reader on the behavior of the information plane in continuous latent spaces.

However, for a one-dimensional Gaussian, the differential entropy is $H = \frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi e \sigma^2)$. Setting $H=0$ implies $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi e}$. The notation $I_{[k]}$ presumably denotes the $k$-dimensional identity matrix. While the intent is clear, the notation 'zero-entropy noise' is technically a misnomer if the noise distribution has a non-zero covariance (which it must to be Gaussian). It would be more precise to refer to it as 'unit-entropy-minimizing noise' or similar, as the noise itself has entropy, and the claim 'we obtain $H[Z] \ge H[\epsilon] = 0$' is only tautological if the entropy of the noise $\epsilon$ is exactly zero. While adding noise helps avoid the pathological negative infinite entropy of continuous $Z$, the equality of these quantities does not generally hold for continuous variables even with noise. Specifically, $I[X;Z|Y] = H[Z|Y] - H[Z|X,Y]$. The inequality $I[X;Z|Y] \le H[Z|Y]$ holds generally (since mutual information is entropy minus conditional entropy, and conditional entropy is non-negative), but the text suggests that because they have 'enforced non-negativity', the standard I-diagram relations now apply. The notation $1/(2\pi e)$ is ambiguous regarding dimensionality; usually, 'zero-entropy' implies setting the variance such that the entropy sum is zero, which depends on the dimension $k$.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript presents a theoretically grounded unification of Information Bottleneck (IB) objectives, effectively bridging abstract information-theoretic principles with practical deep learning optimization by deriving surrogate losses that circumvent the need for complex density estimation or variational approximations. The work's primary strength lies in its clarity and the insightful mapping of competing IB formulations to standard training components, particularly the rigorous derivation showing that decoder uncertainty corresponds to cross-entropy loss augmented with dropout regularization, which provides a valuable justification for widely used practices. However, the most significant issue is the perceived gap between theoretical novelty and practical contribution; since the proposed surrogates largely recover standard objectives, the authors must compellingly articulate the unique value of the IB perspective beyond validating existing methods. Reviewers will likely demand stronger empirical evidence that the IB-derived modifications, such as the Gaussian noise injection for optimizing reverse decoder uncertainty, yield tangible performance or robustness gains over well-understood baselines like CE+Dropout, rather than merely reproducing IB-like trajectories. Additionally, while the noise injection handles differential entropy challenges, it reintroduces estimation biases that could distort the information plane, a limitation that warrants a

comments.title: Trivial/Typographical Error in Proposition 2 Inequality
comments.quote: Proposition 2. _The Decoder Cross-Entropy provides an upper bound on the Decoder Uncertainty:_ H[Y | Z] ≤ H[Y | Z] + _D_ KL(p( _y_ | _z_ ) || pθ(ŷ | _z_ )) = Hθ[Y | Z]
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the inequality comparing H[Y|Z] to itself plus a non-negative KL divergence. I checked the surrounding text and Section D.2 (referenced in the caption) to see if context resolves the notation, and I confirmed the authors intend to bound the true conditional entropy by the model's cross-entropy. However, the formal statement in the proposition literally writes H[Y|Z] ≤ H[Y|Z] + D_KL(...), which is trivially true regardless of the model and obscures the actual bounding relationship. The left-hand side should distinguish the true entropy (e.g., H_true[Y|Z] or H_s[Y|Z]) from the surrogate. This typo misrepresents the proposition's mathematical claim and could mislead readers about what is actually being bounded.

comments.title: Garbled Notation and Unclear Parameter Equivalence in Proposition 1
comments.explanation: The OCR corruption renders the definitions for β′ and β′′ completely unreadable, completely obscuring the exact mathematical mapping between the original IB/DIB hyperparameter β and the surrogate coefficients. Combined with the lack of transparency in the algebraic equivalence shown in the proposition, this creates a localized clarity and reproducibility gap. While deferring the derivation to Appendix C.3 is acceptable, reconstructing the most plausible parameters does not fully resolve the ambiguity without the explicit mathematical forms. The unreadable notation and implicit transformations make it difficult to verify how the regularization strength scales relative to the accuracy term.

comments.title: Overstated Claim Regarding Standard Training and Decoder Uncertainty
comments.quote: We conclude this section by highlighting that H[Y | Z] is therefore already minimized in modern DNN architectures that use dropout together with a cross-entropy loss.
comments.explanation: Initially, this claim seemed to contradict standard deep learning practice, where cross-entropy directly optimizes H_θ[Y|X], not H[Y|Z]. I checked Section 3.2 and Corollary 1 to understand the authors' intent, and I recognized they are using dropout to show that standard training implicitly minimizes an upper bound (H_θ[Y|Z]) on the target quantity. The intent is sound and correctly motivates their surrogate formulation. However, stating that H[Y|Z] itself is 'already minimized' conflates the optimization objective with the information-theoretic quantity it approximates. The phrasing overstates the direct correspondence and could mislead readers into thinking standard training explicitly optimizes the conditional entropy. A minor reframing to 'implicitly minimizes an upper bound on H[Y|Z]' would align the claim more precisely with the mathematical development.

comments.title: Potentially misleading claim about I-diagram sign constraints
comments.quote: For categorical Z, all the quantities in the diagram are positive, which allows us to read off inequalities from the diagram: only I[X; Y; Z] could be negative, but as Y and Z are independent given X, we have I[Y; Z|X] = 0, and I[X; Y; Z] = I[Y; Z]−I[Y; Z|X] = I[Y; Z] ≥ 0.
comments.explanation: The authors state that 'only I[X; Y; Z] could be negative' before immediately proving it equals I[Y; Z] ≥ 0. While technically accurate in the broader context of generalized signed measures for information diagrams (Yeung 1991), it momentarily contradicts the standard Venn-diagram intuition that all marginals, conditionals, and intersections are non-negative for discrete/categorical variables. A novice reader might pause here, though the immediate clarification resolves the concern. Minor phrasing improvement suggested for smoother pedagogical flow.

comments.title: Loose characterization of Jensen's inequality equality condition
comments.quote: Jensen’s inequality yields Hθ[Y | X] ≤ Hθ[Y | Z], with equality iff Z is a deterministic function of X .
comments.explanation: The statement initially raised a concern because equality in Jensen's inequality (E_Z[-log p] = -log E_Z[p]) holds whenever -log p_θ(Ŷ=y|z) is constant almost everywhere over the support of z. While it is true that a deterministic Z=f(X) collapses the expectation to a point mass (satisfying equality), the strict converse is not true: equality would also hold if the decoder p_θ(Ŷ=y|z) completely ignored Z (i.e., p_θ(Ŷ=y|z) = p_θ(Ŷ=y|X) regardless of Z). In the context of training informative representations, the authors likely implicitly assume the decoder must utilize Z, making the claim pragmatically acceptable. However, mathematically, the "iff" condition is slightly too restrictive.

comments.title: Ambiguous Phrasing and Semantic Imprecision in Corollary 1 Regarding Upper Bound Estimation
comments.quote: Then h <pθ( Ŷ = _y_ | _z_ = fθ( _x_ ; η))>_ evaluated for a single sample_ η _is an unbiased estimator of the Decoder Cross-Entropy_ Hθ[Y | Z] _, and an estimator of an upper bound on the Prediction CrossEntropy_ Hθ[Y | X]._
comments.explanation: The phrase "estimator of an upper bound" is semantically imprecise; the random variable is an unbiased estimator of the _value_ H_θ[Y|Z], which happens to be a fixed constant that upper-bounds H_θ[Y|X]. It is not an estimator _of_ the upper bound itself. Additionally, while the _expectation_ of this single-sample evaluation is guaranteed to upper-bound H_θ[Y|X] via Jensen's inequality, individual samples fluctuate around the surrogate loss and are not pointwise guaranteed to exceed the prediction cross-entropy. While common shorthand in stochastic optimization, clarifying that the expectation provides the theoretical upper bound would eliminate both the semantic ambiguity and potential misinterpretation of per-sample guarantees.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim on guaranteed non-negativity of differential entropy via Gaussian noise
comments.quote: the differential entropy then satisfies H[Z] = H[Ẑ + ε] ≥ H[ε]; and by using zero-entropy noise ε ~ N(0, 2^(1/π_e^I_k)) specifically, we obtain H[Z] ≥ 0.
comments.explanation: The authors propose adding independent zero-entropy Gaussian noise ε to Ẑ to enforce H[Z] ≥ 0. While adding independent noise generally increases differential entropy, the strict inequality H(Ẑ + ε) ≥ H(ε) is not universally guaranteed for arbitrary continuous Ẑ that may have large negative differential entropy. By the Entropy Power Inequality, this generally requires conditions on H(Ẑ). The authors appear to be using this noise addition as a practical regularization mechanism to prevent underflow and bound optimization trajectories, rather than as a provable lower bound for all possible learned representations. Clarifying it as a practical design choice for stabilizing training rather than a strict theoretical guarantee would improve precision.

comments.title: Imprecise claim in Theorem 1 regarding what the surrogate objective bounds
comments.quote: Then each of the following objectives is an estimator of an upper bound on the IB objective.
comments.explanation: Initially, I read this claim and wondered whether the authors meant the surrogate strictly bounds the original MI-formulated IB objective min I[X;Z] - βI[Y;Z]. Upon checking Proposition 1 and the surrounding reformulations, I realized the surrogate H_θ[Y|Z] + γR(Z) upper-bounds the _reformulated_ objective H[Y|Z] + β' H[Z|Y] (via Propositions 2 and 4), not the original IB objective value. While the argmin equivalence is standard in IB literature, claiming the surrogate bounds the IB objective directly conflates the value bounds of the transformed problem with the original MI formulation. The surrogate does not mathematically upper-bound I[X;Z] - βI[Y;Z]. This is a moderate logical issue that could mislead readers about the exact theoretical guarantee and should be clarified as bounding the _reformulated_ IB objective.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of Figure 1 trajectories
comments.quote: See the figure 1, which shows the trajectories on the test set.
comments.explanation: Upon reading this sentence, I initially questioned whether the authors computed information quantities (like H[Z] or I[Y;Z]) over a held-out test set, which would be statistically unstable and highly atypical for mutual information / entropy tracking. However, checking the caption for Figure 1 immediately resolves the intent: it explicitly states 'Information plane plot of the training trajectories' and notes that 'transparency [shows] the training epoch'. In Information Bottleneck literature, these plots are standardly used to visualize optimization dynamics (compression vs. relevant information) during training. Stating that the plot shows 'test set trajectories' directly contradicts the figure caption and established methodology. While the _test accuracy_ axis likely comes from a validation/test split, the information-plane path itself tracks training epochs. This is likely a phrasing oversight, but it creates a localized contradiction that could mislead readers about whether compression dynamics and generalization are evaluated on the same data split or conflated.

comments.title: Inequality stated as equality in Proposition 3
comments.quote: After adding zero-entropy noise, the inequality I[X; Z | Y] ≤ H[Z | Y] ≤ H[Z] also holds for continuous Z
comments.explanation: The authors state the chain of inequalities I[X; Z | Y] ≤ H[Z | Y] ≤ H[Z]. While mathematically true, the first relation is actually an equality under the paper's model assumptions. Since Z is generated conditionally only on X (via the encoder), conditioning on both X and Y does not change the entropy of Z, meaning H[Z | X, Y] = H[Z | X] = H[ε] = 0. Consequently, I[X; Z | Y] = H[Z | Y] - H[Z | X, Y] = H[Z | Y]. Stating it as a non-strict inequality is technically valid but slightly imprecise and may mislead readers into thinking there is an actual bounding gap where none exists. This does not affect the optimization logic but is worth clarifying for theoretical rigor.

comments.title: Oversimplified derivation linking KL divergence to cross-entropy
comments.quote: We can derive the cross-entropy loss H(ˆp( _y_ | _x_ ) || pθ(Y[ˆ] = _y_ | _x_ )) (Solla et al., 1988; Hinton, 1990) by minimizing the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the empirical sample distribution ˆp( _x_ , _y_ ) and the parameterized distribution pθ( _x_ ) pθ(ˆ _y_ | _x_ ), where we set pθ( _x_ ) = ˆp( _x_ ).
comments.explanation: Expanding the KL divergence yields D_KL(ˆp(x,y) || p_θ(x)p_θ(ŷ|x)) = D_KL(ˆp(x) || p_θ(x)) + H(ˆp(y|x)) + H(ˆp(y|x) || p_θ(ŷ|x)). Setting p_θ(x) = ˆp(x) eliminates the first term, leaving the label entropy H(ˆp(y|x)) plus the cross-entropy. The text implies a direct derivation of the cross-entropy loss, but mathematically it minimizes the cross-entropy up to an additive constant (the label entropy) that is invariant to model parameters. While standard practice in ML literature, omitting this constant makes the derivation appear algebraically exact rather than equivalent up to a constant, which could mislead readers expecting a strict identity.

comments.title: Loose characterization of Jensen's inequality equality condition
comments.quote: Jensen’s inequality yields Hθ[Y | X] ≤ Hθ[Y | Z], with equality iff Z is a deterministic function of X .
comments.explanation: The statement initially raised a concern because equality in Jensen's inequality ($\mathbb{E}_{Z}[-\log p] = -\log \mathbb{E}_{Z}[p]$) holds whenever $-\log p_\theta(\hat{Y}=y|z)$ is constant almost everywhere over the support of $z$. While it is true that a deterministic $Z=f(X)$ collapses the expectation to a point mass (satisfying equality), the strict converse is not true: equality would also hold if the decoder $p_\theta(\hat{Y}=y|z)$ completely ignored $Z$ (i.e., $p_\theta(\hat{Y}=y|z) = p_\theta(\hat{Y}=y|X)$ regardless of $Z$). In the context of training informative representations, the authors likely implicitly assume the decoder must utilize $Z$, making the claim pragmatically acceptable. However, mathematically, the "iff" condition is slightly too restrictive.

comments.title: Semantically imprecise phrasing regarding estimation of an upper bound
comments.quote: is an unbiased estimator of the Decoder Cross-Entropy Hθ[Y | Z], and an estimator of an upper bound on the Prediction CrossEntropy Hθ[Y | X].
comments.explanation: The authors correctly leverage Jensen’s inequality to show $H_\theta[Y|Z] \ge H_\theta[Y|X]$. However, stating that a single-sample Monte Carlo value is an "estimator of an upper bound" is imprecise. The random variable $-\log p_\theta(\hat{Y}=y|z)$ is an unbiased estimator of the *value* $H_\theta[Y|Z]$, which happens to be a fixed constant that upper-bounds $H_\theta[Y|X]$. It is not an estimator *of* the upper bound itself (which is a target quantity), but rather an estimator of the bounding quantity. This minor ambiguity could confuse readers about whether the optimization explicitly targets the upper bound or the surrogate loss.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim on guaranteed non-negativity of differential entropy via Gaussian noise
comments.quote: the differential entropy then satisfies H[Z] = H[ˆZ + ϵ] ≥ H[ϵ]; and by using zero-entropy noise ϵ ~ N(0, 21π _e[I][k]_[) specifically, we obtain H[Z] ≥ 0.
comments.explanation: The authors propose adding independent zero-entropy Gaussian noise $\epsilon$ to $\hat{Z}$ to enforce $H[Z] \ge 0$. While adding independent noise generally increases differential entropy, the strict inequality $H(\hat{Z} + \epsilon) \ge H(\epsilon)$ is not universally guaranteed for arbitrary continuous $\hat{Z}$ that may have large negative differential entropy. By the Entropy Power Inequality, $H(\hat{Z}+\epsilon) \ge H(\epsilon)$ generally requires $H(\hat{Z}) \ge 0$ or similar conditions. The authors appear to be using this noise addition as a practical regularization mechanism to prevent underflow and bound optimization trajectories, rather than as a provable lower bound for all possible learned representations. This is a minor mathematical looseness; clarifying it as a practical design choice for stabilizing training rather than a strict theoretical guarantee would improve precision.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This manuscript offers a timely and mathematically rigorous contribution to the Information Bottleneck (IB) literature, effectively addressing the practical gap between the theoretical elegance of IB and the difficulty of optimizing its associated information-theoretic objectives. The introduction is exceptionally clear, successfully framing the paper’s contributions by unifying disparate IB formulations through information diagrams and identifying specific quantities—Decoder Uncertainty and Reverse Decoder Uncertainty—that govern optimization. However, a primary concern arising from the proposal is the use of heuristic interventions, such as injecting Gaussian noise to circumvent differential entropy pathologies; while the authors present this as a necessary technical trick, rigorous justification is required to ensure it does not fundamentally alter the underlying distribution properties intended to be captured. Furthermore, the paper makes a bold empirical claim that these simple surrogates reproduce the classic information plane trajectories of Tishby et al. (2015); while the premise is intriguing, this connection requires careful, detailed experimental validation in the main body to prove that the surrogates capture the *principle* rather than just mimicking the surface-level behavior.

comments.title: Mathematical validity and readability issues in Proposition 1
comments.explanation: The paper claims an equivalence between standard information-theoretic objectives and reformulated uncertainty-based objectives in Proposition 1. However, the DIB objective equation appears to contain a mathematical inconsistency: the text states minimality is achieved by minimizing $I[X; Z]$, yet the equation provided is $min H[Z]$. These are not equivalent. Consequently, the text claims 'arg min H[Z] - βI[Y; Z] = arg min H[Y | Z] + β' H[Z | Y]', but this derivation is non-trivial and likely stems from the erroneous DIB definition. Furthermore, the definitions of the bridging parameters $ eta'$ and $ eta''$ are corrupted by OCR (e.g., `β[′] := β−11...`), rendering the parameters unreadable and the mathematical equivalence impossible to verify.

comments.title: Corrupted noise variance definition
comments.quote: $\epsilon \sim N(0, 21\pi _e[I][k]_[)$
comments.explanation: The mathematical definition for the zero-entropy noise distribution is corrupted by OCR artifacts (brackets, underlines, and garbled Unicode characters like '[I]'). Even assuming the intended value, the presented string makes the variance parameter unintelligible and likely contradictory to the claim that the noise induces zero differential entropy.

comments.title: Approximation presented as equality in dimension calculation
comments.quote: 224 × 224 × 3 = 1.5 × 10[5] input dimensions
comments.explanation: The equation presents a precise calculation ($224 	imes 224 	imes 3 = 150,528$) and equates it to a rounded approximation ($1.5 	imes 10^5 = 150,000$). While the approximation is acceptable for scaling discussions, presenting it as an equation introduces a precision error.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of DIB objective
comments.quote: min I[X; Z] − βI[Y; Z] for IB, and min H[Z] − βI[Y; Z] for DIB.
comments.explanation: The introduction to Section 3.1 states, 'Minimality is achieved by minimizing the Preserved Information I[X; Z]'. Minimizing $H[Z]$ alone is not equivalent to minimizing $I[X; Z]$. The equation for DIB seems to be a typo for the IB objective (or the standard DIB objective which minimizes $H(Z|X)$ or $I(X;Z)$). The stated objective 'min H[Z] - beta I[Y; Z]' minimizes entropy $H[Z]$ while maximizing information about Y, which differs from the definition of DIB and contradicts the text's explanation of the IB principle.

comments.title: Inconsistent entropy notation
comments.quote: We denote entropy H[·]... We will use differential entropies interchangeably with entropies... In Section 3.1... Proposition 1. ... = arg min H[Y | Z] + β' H[Z | Y] ...
comments.explanation: Section 2 explicitly defines the notation as $H[\cdot]$ (square brackets). However, in the subsequent text and Proposition 1, the authors use parentheses $(\cdot)$ (e.g., $H[Y | Z]$, $H[Z | Y]$). While this is a common and acceptable convention in the field to distinguish between random variables and values, the authors had explicitly defined their notation in the preamble. The inconsistency makes the text slightly harder to parse strictly according to the established rules.

comments.title: Questionable equivalence in Proposition 1
comments.quote: arg min H[Z] − βI[Y; Z] = arg min H[Y | Z] + β' H[Z | Y] = arg min H[Y | Z] + β'' H[Z]
comments.explanation: The text claims that the DIB objective 'min H[Z] − βI[Y; Z]' is reformulated as 'min H[Y | Z] + β' H[Z | Y]'. Since $H[Y | Z]$ is reconstruction error (to be minimized) and $H[Z | Y]$ is redundancy (to be minimized), and the form 'min H[Z] - beta I[Y; Z]' (when $ eta=1$) simplifies to 'min H[Z|Y]', the presence of $H[Y|Z]$ in the reformulation is mathematically non-trivial and not self-evident without the derivation in section C.3. However, given the likely typo in the DIB objective equation noted above, this specific line of reasoning in Proposition 1 should be verified, as the premise seems incorrect.

comments.title: Proposition 1 definitions are unreadable due to OCR failure
comments.explanation: In Proposition 1 (Equation 6), the text attempts to define parameters β′ and β″ that bridge the IB and DIB objectives to the reformulated uncertainty-based objectives. However, the LaTeX rendering is severely corrupted by OCR. The string `β−11[∈][[0][,][ ∞)[ ]` is unintelligible and likely represents a corrupted mathematical expression (e.g., `β := β + 1` or `β ∈ [0, ∞)`). Similarly, `β[1][∈][[0][,][ 1)[ ]` is garbled. Without the correct mathematical definitions, it is impossible to verify whether the stated equalities `arg min H[Z] - βI[Y;Z] = arg min H[Y|Z] + β′ H[Z|Y] = arg min H[Y|Z] + β″ H[Z]` hold true. The correctness of this equivalence is critical to the paper's central claim that standard objectives can replace complex information-theoretic ones.

comments.title: Missing text in Proposition 1 regarding IB objective
comments.quote: **Proposition 1.** _For IB, we obtain_ _and, for DIB,_
comments.explanation: The text begins the proposition by stating 'For IB, we obtain' followed immediately by 'and, for DIB,'. This implies a line of text is missing between these two clauses. Contextually, the IB objective is `min I[X; Z] - βI[Y; Z]`, whereas the DIB objective is `min H[Z] - βI[Y; Z]`. The paper discusses reformulating the DIB objective (starting with H[Z]) but omits the derivation or text for reformulating the IB objective. This creates a disjointed reading experience and raises a question about whether the IB reformulation follows a different logic or path, which is not addressed.

comments.title: Garbled notation in noise variance definition
comments.quote: $\epsilon \sim N(0, 21\pi _e[I][k]_[)$
comments.explanation: The passage contains a corrupted mathematical definition for the zero-entropy noise distribution, specifically in the variance parameter. The notation "$21\pi _e[I][k]_[)$" includes uninterpretable artifacts such as "[I]", brackets, and underlines. Even assuming the most plausible intended symbol (e.g., a variance of $1/(2\pi e k)$ or similar to induce unit entropy), the current text is completely unreadable and likely mathematically inconsistent (a large variance value would contradict the claim that this noise has zero differential entropy $H[\epsilon] = 0$).

comments.title: Misinterpretation of inequality validity via noise
comments.quote: Proposition 3. After adding zero-entropy noise, the inequality I[X; Z | Y] <= H[Z | Y] <= H[Z] also holds for continuous Z
comments.explanation: The text suggests that the inequality $H[Z|Y] \le H[Z]$ (derived from the non-negativity of mutual information $I[Z;Y]$) only holds or becomes valid after adding noise. However, this inequality is a fundamental identity of information theory ($h(Z) = h(Z|Y) + I(Z;Y)$) that holds for all pairs of random variables, regardless of whether they are discrete or continuous. The noise is strictly necessary to ensure these entropies are non-negative and bounded from below, preventing optimization paths that collapse variances, but it is not mathematically required for the validity of the inequalities themselves.

comments.title: OCR Artifacts in Exponent Notation
comments.explanation: The notation uses `10[5]`, which appears to be an OCR error for `10^5`. While the intent is clearly scientific notation to indicate one-hundred thousand, the symbol `[5]` is inconsistent with standard mathematical notation and the surrounding context of scientific writing. Assuming the intended symbol is $10^5$ is necessary to make sense of the sentence, but the text itself contains an obvious notational mistake.

comments.title: Inexact Equality in Dimension Calculation
comments.quote: 224 × 224 × 3 = 1.5 × 10[5] input dimensions
comments.explanation: The text presents an equation asserting an exact equality ($224 	imes 224 	imes 3 = 150,528$) with a rounded value ($1.5 	imes 10^5 = 150,000$). While this is an acceptable approximation in a high-level summary comparing dataset scales (e.g., to CIFAR-10's 3072 dimensions), presenting it as an equation suggests a precision that does not exist. The exact flattened input dimension is $1.505 	imes 10^5$.
